title: The Man With the Keys
summary: A school caretaker in Scotland is invisible to most people, but he remembers every child who ever cried in his corridor.
body:
Tom is the caretaker at a primary school in Glasgow, Scotland. He arrives at six in the morning, before anyone else. He opens the doors, turns on the heating, and cleans the floors. Most children do not know his name. To them he is just "the man with the keys."

But Tom knows them. He knows which child is afraid of the dark stairs. He knows who eats alone and who never has a coat in winter. When a small boy cried in the corridor last year, Tom did not call a teacher. He sat down next to him and waited. He gave the boy a tissue and said nothing. After ten minutes, the boy felt better and went back to class.

Tom has fixed thousands of broken chairs. He has cleaned up paint, mud, and tears. He earns less than the teachers, and his name is not on the school website. Yet he believes his job matters. "A school is not only books," he says. "It is also a warm room and a clean floor and someone who notices when you are sad."

This year Tom will retire. The headteacher planned a small party, but the children planned a bigger one. They made hundreds of cards. One card said, "Thank you for always smiling at me." Tom read it three times. He had thought he was invisible. Now he understood that the children had seen him all along. They knew his face, even if they never learned his name.

annotations.meaningVi: người trông coi, bảo vệ trường
người đàn ông giữ chìa khóa
đưa cho cậu bé một tờ khăn giấy
kiếm được ít hơn các giáo viên
công việc của ông ấy quan trọng

annotations.phrase: someone who notices when you are sad
annotations.meaningVi: ai đó nhận ra khi bạn buồn
ông ấy vô hình (không ai để ý)
đã luôn nhìn thấy ông ấy

grammarNotes.title: Present simple for daily routine
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thì hiện tại đơn diễn tả các việc làm thường ngày, lặp đi lặp lại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use the present simple for routine: 'He opens the doors, turns on the heating, and cleans the floors.'

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with quantities
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nói về tổng số việc đã làm trong một khoảng thời gian dài đến nay.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Tom has fixed thousands of broken chairs' uses the present perfect to count work done over many years.

grammarNotes.title: Future with 'will'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'will' + động từ nguyên thể để nói về một sự việc trong tương lai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use 'will' for the future in 'This year Tom will retire.'

translationVi:
Tom là người bảo vệ tại một trường tiểu học ở Glasgow, Scotland. Ông đến lúc sáu giờ sáng, trước bất kỳ ai khác. Ông mở cửa, bật hệ thống sưởi và lau sàn nhà. Hầu hết bọn trẻ không biết tên ông. Với chúng, ông chỉ là "người đàn ông giữ chìa khóa."

Nhưng Tom biết chúng. Ông biết đứa trẻ nào sợ cầu thang tối. Ông biết ai ăn một mình và ai không bao giờ có áo khoác vào mùa đông. Khi một cậu bé khóc ở hành lang năm ngoái, Tom đã không gọi giáo viên. Ông ngồi xuống cạnh cậu và chờ đợi. Ông đưa cho cậu bé một tờ khăn giấy và không nói gì. Sau mười phút, cậu bé cảm thấy đỡ hơn và quay lại lớp.

Tom đã sửa hàng nghìn chiếc ghế hỏng. Ông đã dọn sạch sơn, bùn và nước mắt. Ông kiếm được ít hơn các giáo viên, và tên ông không có trên trang web của trường. Tuy vậy, ông tin rằng công việc của mình quan trọng. "Một ngôi trường không chỉ là sách vở," ông nói. "Nó còn là một căn phòng ấm áp, một sàn nhà sạch sẽ và ai đó nhận ra khi bạn buồn."

Năm nay Tom sẽ nghỉ hưu. Thầy hiệu trưởng đã lên kế hoạch cho một bữa tiệc nhỏ, nhưng bọn trẻ lại lên kế hoạch cho một bữa lớn hơn. Chúng làm hàng trăm tấm thiệp. Một tấm thiệp viết: "Cảm ơn ông vì luôn mỉm cười với con." Tom đọc nó ba lần. Ông từng nghĩ mình vô hình. Giờ ông hiểu rằng bọn trẻ đã luôn nhìn thấy ông. Chúng biết khuôn mặt ông, dù chúng chưa bao giờ biết tên ông.

questions.explanation: 'Tom is the caretaker at a primary school in Glasgow, Scotland.'

questions.prompt: What time does Tom arrive at school?
questions.explanation: 'He arrives at six in the morning, before anyone else.'

questions.prompt: What do most children call Tom?
questions.explanation: 'To them he is just "the man with the keys."'
questions.hint: Look at the end of the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: What did Tom do when the small boy cried in the corridor?
options: He sent him to the headteacher
He told him to stop crying
He sat next to him and waited quietly
questions.explanation: 'He sat down next to him and waited. He gave the boy a tissue and said nothing.'

questions.prompt: How much does Tom earn compared with the teachers?
questions.explanation: 'He earns less than the teachers, and his name is not on the school website.'

questions.prompt: According to Tom, what is a school besides books?
options: A warm room and someone who notices when you are sad
questions.explanation: 'A school is not only books... It is also a warm room and a clean floor and someone who notices when you are sad.'
questions.hint: Read Tom's own words in paragraph three.

questions.prompt: In the text, what does 'invisible' mean?
questions.explanation: Tom thought he was invisible, but the cards showed the children had 'seen him all along', so invisible means unnoticed.
questions.hint: Look at the last paragraph, near 'seen him all along'.

questions.prompt: Why did the children make hundreds of cards?
options: Because Tom will retire this year
questions.explanation: 'This year Tom will retire... the children planned a bigger one. They made hundreds of cards.'
questions.hint: Look at the start of the last paragraph.

questions.prompt: What can we guess about how the children really felt about Tom?
options: They cared about him even though they did not know his name
They never noticed him at all
questions.explanation: One card said 'Thank you for always smiling at me,' and the text says 'the children had seen him all along.'
questions.hint: Think about what the cards reveal.

questions.prompt: What is the main message of the passage?
options: Quiet, ordinary workers can matter deeply to others
questions.explanation: The story shows Tom's small kind acts mattered to the children even though his role seemed invisible.
questions.hint: Think about the overall point of the story.

questions.prompt: How did Tom feel after reading the card that thanked him for smiling?
options: Surprised that the children had really seen him
questions.explanation: 'He had thought he was invisible. Now he understood that the children had seen him all along.'
questions.hint: Look at his reaction at the very end.

blanks.explanation: He arrives at six in the morning.
'Most children do not know his name.'
He knows which child is afraid of the dark stairs.
'When a small boy cried in the corridor last year...'
'He gave the boy a tissue and said nothing.'
After ten minutes the boy felt better.
'Tom has fixed thousands of broken chairs.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The card said 'Thank you for always smiling at me,' and Tom thought he was invisible. Who are the 'invisible' workers in your own life that you rarely thank, and why do we often fail to notice them?
